verses.sanskrit: कर्मण्येवाधिकारस्ते मा फलेषु कदाचन | मा कर्मफलहेतुर्भूर्मा ते सङ्गोऽस्त्वकर्मणि ||
verses.translation: You have a right to perform your prescribed duties, but you are not entitled to the fruits of your actions. Never consider yourself to be the cause of the results of your activities, and never be attached to not doing your duty.
verses.simpleMeaning: Focus entirely on the process and the quality of your work, rather than obsessing over the outcome or reward.
practicalSituations: Fear of failure in a new project
reflectionPrompts: How much of my current stress is coming from a result I cannot control?
What is the very next quality act I can perform right now?

verses.translation: O son of Kunti, the nonpermanent appearance of happiness and distress, and their disappearance in due course, are like the appearance and disappearance of winter and summer seasons. They arise from sense perception, O scion of Bharata, and one must learn to tolerate them without being disturbed.
verses.simpleMeaning: Emotions and external situations are seasonal. Like winter turns to summer, your current pain or joy is temporary.
reflectionPrompts: Can I treat this current emotion as a passing weather pattern?
What remained constant in me before this situation began?

verses.sanskrit: ध्यायतो विषयान्पुंस: सङ्गस्तेषूपजायते | सङ्गात्सञ्जायते काम: कामात्क्रोधोऽभिजायते ||
verses.translation: While contemplating the objects of the senses, a person develops attachment for them, and from such attachment lust develops, and from lust anger arises.
verses.simpleMeaning: Obsessing over what you want creates attachment. When that attachment is blocked, it inevitably turns into rage.
practicalSituations: Losing temper when things don't go your way
reflectionPrompts: What specific expectation of mine was just blocked?
Is my anger a reaction to a loss of control?

verses.sanskrit: उद्धरेदात्मनात्मानं नात्मानमवसादयेत् | आत्मैव ह्य़ात्मनो बन्धुरात्मैव रिपुरात्मन: ||
verses.translation: Elevate yourself through your own mind, and do not degrade yourself. For the mind can be the friend and also the enemy of the self.
verses.simpleMeaning: You are your own greatest ally or your own worst critic. Your internal monologue determines your reality.
reflectionPrompts: Is the voice in my head currently coaching me or bullying me?
What is one small way I can be a friend to myself today?

verses.sanskrit: इति ते ज्ञानमाख्यातं गुह्याद्गुह्यतरं मया | विमृश्यैतदशेषेण यथेच्छसि तथा कुरु ||
verses.translation: Thus I have explained to you the knowledge that is still more confidential. Deliberate on this fully, and then do what you wish to do.
verses.simpleMeaning: Wisdom is given as a tool for reflection, but the final choice and responsibility are always yours.
reflectionPrompts: Am I acting out of my own conviction or out of fear of others?
Have I weighed all perspectives before deciding?

verses.translation: One whose mind remains undisturbed amidst misery, who does not crave for pleasure, and who is free from attachment, fear, and anger, is called a sage of steady wisdom.
verses.simpleMeaning: True peace isn't the absence of trouble, but the presence of internal stability regardless of external chaos.
practicalSituations: Not getting over-excited by temporary praise
reflectionPrompts: Could I experience this difficulty without adding a layer of mental distress to it?
Where is the 'steady point' inside me right now?

verses.sanskrit: काम एष क्रोध एष रजोगुणसमुद्भव: | महाशनो महापाप्मा विद्ध्येनमिह वैरिणम् ||
verses.translation: It is lust only, which is born of contact with the material mode of passion and later transformed into wrath, and which is the all-devouring sinful enemy of this world.
verses.simpleMeaning: Unchecked desire is like a fire; it consumes everything and eventually turns into destructive anger.
reflectionPrompts: Is this desire serving me, or am I serving it?
What would happen if I sat with this urge for 10 minutes without acting on it?

verses.sanskrit: न हि ज्ञानेन सदृशं पवित्रमिह विद्यते | तत्स्वयं योगसंसिद्ध: कालेनात्मनि विन्दति ||
verses.translation: In this world, there is nothing so sublime and pure as transcendental knowledge. One who has become accomplished in the practice of yoga enjoys this knowledge within himself in due course of time.
verses.simpleMeaning: Knowledge and self-awareness are the ultimate purifiers of the mind. Direct experience takes time.
reflectionPrompts: What is the truth of this situation that I am currently ignoring?
Am I willing to wait for understanding to ripen?

verses.sanskrit: यस्मान्नोद्विजते लोको लोकान्नोद्विजते च य: | हर्षामर्षभयोद्वेगैर्मुक्तो य: स च मे प्रिय: ||
verses.translation: He by whom the world is not agitated and who cannot be agitated by the world, who is freed from pleasure, envy, fear and anxiety, is very dear to Me.
verses.simpleMeaning: Integrity is being someone who neither disturbs others nor allows their own peace to be disturbed by others.
reflectionPrompts: Am I reacting to someone else's chaos with my own?
How can I be a source of calm in this room?

verses.translation: Satisfaction, simplicity, gravity, self-control and purification of one's existence are the austerities of the mind.
verses.simpleMeaning: Real discipline isn't physical torture—it’s keeping the mind clean, kind, and quiet.
reflectionPrompts: What would 'simplicity of mind' look like in this complex problem?
How can I practice silence right now?

verses.sanskrit: क्लैब्यं मा स्म गम: पार्थ नैतत्त्वय्युपपद्यते | क्षुद्रं हृदयदौर्बल्यं त्यक्त्वोत्तिष्ठ परन्तप ||
verses.translation: O son of Pritha, do not yield to this degrading impotence. It does not become you. Give up such petty weakness of heart and arise, O chastiser of the enemy.
verses.simpleMeaning: Do not give in to paralyzing self-pity or fear. It is beneath your true potential. Stand up and face your duty.
reflectionPrompts: Is my current hesitation based on wisdom or just 'weakness of heart'?
What is the courageous path here?

verses.sanskrit: त्रिविधं नरकस्येदं द्वारं नाशनमात्मन: | काम: क्रोधस्तथा लोभस्तस्मादेतत्त्रयं त्यजेत् ||
verses.translation: There are three gates leading to this hell—lust, anger and greed. Every sane man should give these up, for they lead to the degradation of the soul.
verses.simpleMeaning: Lust (compulsion), Anger (reaction), and Greed (excess) are the three traps that destroy a person's peace and character.
reflectionPrompts: Which of these three 'gates' am I standing near right now?
How would removing this impulse change my outlook?

verses.sanskrit: सहजं कर्म कौन्तेय सदोषमपि न त्यजेत् | सर्वारम्भा हि दोषेण धूमेनाग्निरिवावृता: ||
verses.translation: Every endeavor is covered by some fault, just as fire is covered by smoke. Therefore one should not give up the work born of his nature, O son of Kunti, even if such work is full of fault.
verses.simpleMeaning: Perfection is a myth. Every path has its flaws. Don't abandon your purpose just because it feels messy or imperfect.
practicalSituations: Giving up on a hobby because it's hard
reflectionPrompts: Am I seeking a 'smokeless fire'?
Can I accept the flaws of this task while still doing it with heart?

verses.sanskrit: बुद्धियुक्तो जहातीह उभे सुकृतदुष्कृते | तस्माद्योगाय युज्यस्व योग: कर्मसु कौशलम् ||
verses.translation: A man engaged in devotional service rids himself of both good and bad reactions even in this life. Therefore strive for yoga, which is the art of all work.
verses.simpleMeaning: Efficiency and excellence in action define a balanced life. Yoga is essentially the skill of acting with total presence.
reflectionPrompts: How can I turn this mundane chore into a skillful act?
Where is my attention right now?
